subject: Physics
solution:
The area under the velocity time graph gives the displacement. The area for t=0 s to t=25 s is 

(Area)=12×5×10+5×10+12×30×5+12×20×5-12×20×5

 ⇒Area=25+50+75+50-50=150 m
Hence, the displacement =150 m

The distance is defined as the path taken by the object to travel from initial to final position. Also note that displacement is a vector quantity, but distance is a scalar quantity.
Distance =12×5×10+5×10+12×30×5+12×20×5+12×20×5=(200+50)=250 m

Therefore, required ratio
 distance  displacement =250150=53

subject: Physics
solution:
As the juggler is throwing n balls each second and 2nd when the first is at its highest point, so the time taken by one ball to reach the highest point, t=1n s.

And as at highest point v=0.

So, from first equation of motion,

v=u+at⇒0=u-g1n⇒u=gn

Now in order to calculate height, using second equation of motion, we have:
  S=ut+12at2      ...(i)

⇒h=1n×gn-12g×1n2

     ⇒h=g2n2  

So, the balls rise up to a height of h=g2n2 m.

subject: Physics
solution:
When the bullet just enters the target, its equation of motion is given by

v2-u2=2aS   ...1

Equation (1) implies that

2u32=u2+2-a4×10-2⇒4u29=u2-2a4×10-2⇒5u29=2a4×10-2   …2

When the bullet stops inside the target, its equation of motion can be written as

0=2u32+2-ax   ...3

Equation (3) implies that

4u29=2ax   ...4

Dividing equation (2) by equation (4), we have

54=4×10-2x⇒x=165×10-2⇒x=3.2×10-2 m=32×10-3 m

Hence, D=32.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the ball fall a distance $s$ from the initial height.

Initial velocity $u = 0$

Acceleration $a = g = 10$ m/s$^2$

Given that the magnitude of velocity is equal to the magnitude of acceleration due to gravity, we have $v = 10$ m/s.

Using the equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 + 2gs$:

$(10)^2 = 0^2 + 2 \times 10 \times s$

$100 = 20s$

$s = 5$ m

The distance fallen by the ball is $5$ m.

The height above the ground is $h = H - s$

$h = 18 - 5 = 13$ m

Answer: $13$

solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{u}=360 \times \frac{5}{18}=100 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{u} \times \mathrm{t}=2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{H}}{\mathrm{g}}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{H}=\frac{\mathrm{t}^2 \mathrm{~g}}{2} \\ & \mathrm{H}=\frac{400 \times 10}{2} \\ & \mathrm{H}=2000 \mathrm{~m} \\ & \mathrm{D}=\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^2+\mathrm{H}^2} \\ & \mathrm{D}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~km}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the initial velocity of the body be v. Hence the final velocity = v/2
Applying $v^2=u^2-2 a s \Rightarrow\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)^2=v^2-2 \cdot a \cdot 3 \Rightarrow a=v^2 / 8$
In IInd case when the body comes to rest, final velocity = 0, initial velocity = v/2
Again, $(0)^2=\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)^2-2 \cdot \frac{v^2}{8} \cdot s ;$ or $s=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
So the extra penetration will be $1 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
solution:
If the ball was thrown with initial velocity u , then its velocity at height 'h' can be written as.
v=u2-2gh
Its linear momentum, p=mv=mu2-2gh
Or  p2=m2u2-2m2gh
The graph between p & h will be a parabola.
In motion when particle was going up, its velocity is positive and decreasing with height and so is the linear momentum. When it reaches the maximum height, the velocity and linear momentum will be zero. Again, it will start to fall back, the velocity and linear momentum will increase but will have negative value.

subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}^2=1+\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{xv}=\mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}+\mathrm{v} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{dt}}=1 \\ & \mathrm{x} \cdot \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{v}^2=1 \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1-\mathrm{v}^2}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2 / \mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \mathrm{a}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}^3}=\mathrm{x}^{-3}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the total distance be d.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t1=d3v1.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t2=d3v2.

Time taken to cover 13rd of total distance is t3=d3v3.

Average speed=Total distanceTotal time

vavg=dd3v1+d3v2+d3v3

⇒vavg=3v1v2v3v1v2+v2v3+v3v1

Here, v1=11 m s-1, then, v3=33 m s-1, v2=22  m s-1

Vavg =3×11×22×3311×22+22×33+33×11=18 m s-1

subject: Physics
solution:
Speed of an object is defined as the distance travelled by the object per unit time.

Let's assume that, AB= d. So, BC= d, AD= 3d. Also, assume that the time required by the object to travel AB, BC, CD are t1, t2, t3 respectively.

Now,

AD= 3ABAB+BC+CD= 3ABAB+AB+CD= 3ABCD= AB

Hence,

t1= ABv1= dv1t2= dv2t3= dv3

The formula to calculate the average speed va of the object is given by

va= ADt1+t2+t3........................(1)

Substitute the expressions for the parameters into equation (1) and simplify to obtain the required average speed of the object.

va= 3ddv1+dv2+dv3= 3v1v2v3v1v2+v2v3+v3v1

subject: Physics
solution:
Using s=ut+12at2

Initially, u=0, so time taken to reach from A to B is  t=2sg=2×4.99.8=1 s

Now, using v2=u2+2as, we have

v=2gh=2×9.8×4.9=9.8 m s-1 

∵ t2=4-1 s=3 s  

∴  d=t2×v=3×9.8=29.4 m
